content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: if + past perfect simple, would + have + past participle

content.subtitle: Use
rows: Unreal situations in the past and their unreal past results
If the chemist had been open, I would have bought some aspirin.
If I hadn't listened to you, I would have cooked the chicken for too long.
If he had seen the doctor, he wouldn't have been ill for such a long time.
If you hadn't eaten a giant pizza, you wouldn't have been sick.

content.subtitle: Helpful hints
content.description: We can also use **could** and **might** instead of **would**, depending on the meaning.
items: If you had eaten a giant pizza, you **might have been** sick. (possible, but not certain)
If Mary had told me she was coming, I **could have cooked** a nice meal. (ability in the past)

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: The third conditional is the only conditional that refers to the past: **If I had had a headache, I would have taken an aspirin.**
In the second conditional, past simple does not refer to the past: **If I had a headache, I would take an aspirin.**

Second conditional form: **if + past simple, would + bare infinitive** — used for impossible or unlikely situations in the present or future: **If my legs were longer, I would be a much faster runner!**

sections.title: A: Complete the third conditional sentences
sections.instructions: Use the correct form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: If I ...... (know) about the traffic, I ...... (leave) earlier.
If they ...... (study) harder, they ...... (pass) the exam.
If we ...... (not miss) the train, we ...... (arrive) on time.
If she ...... (call) me, I ...... (help) her.
If he ...... (take) an umbrella, he ...... (not get) wet.
If you ...... (not eat) so much, you ...... (not feel) sick.

sections.title: B: Choose would have, could have or might have
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence with the best option.

items.sentence: If I had had your number, I ...... called you immediately.
If we had left earlier, we ...... caught the last bus.
If she had worn a coat, she ...... been warmer.
If they had listened to the weather forecast, they ...... stayed at home.
If I had had more time, I ...... finished the report.
If he had taken that medicine, he ...... felt better by now.

sections.title: C: Error correction
sections.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake. Write the correct form.

items.sentence: If I would have seen you, I would have said hello.
items.answer: If I had seen you, I would have said hello.

items.sentence: If she had went to bed earlier, she wouldn't have been tired.
items.answer: If she had gone to bed earlier, she wouldn't have been tired.

items.sentence: If we had knew the answer, we would have told you.
items.answer: If we had known the answer, we would have told you.

items.sentence: If they hadn't forgot the tickets, they could have entered.
items.answer: If they hadn't forgotten the tickets, they could have entered.

items.sentence: If he had studied, he will have passed.
items.answer: If he had studied, he would have passed.

items.sentence: If you had called me, I can have helped you.
items.answer: If you had called me, I could have helped you.

sections.title: D: Rewrite with the third conditional
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning.

items.sentence:
I didn't set an alarm, so I woke up late. **IF**
If I ...... an alarm, I wouldn't have woken up late.

She didn't check the map, so she got lost. **HAD**
If she ...... the map, she wouldn't have got lost.

We didn't book in advance, so we didn't get seats. **WOULD**
If we had booked in advance, we ...... seats.

Tom forgot his wallet, so he couldn't pay. **COULD**
If Tom hadn't forgotten his wallet, he ...... .

It rained all day, so they didn't play football. **PLAYED**
If it hadn't rained all day, they ...... football.

I ate too much at lunch, so I felt sleepy. **FELT**
If I hadn't eaten so much at lunch, I ...... sleepy.
